title: Solar 2026 mid-year outlook
gist: Wood Mackenzie's H1 2026 solar mid-year review finds demand forecasts revised upward again, silver costs surging 30%+ into TOPCon modules, and geopolitical shocks from the Strait of Hormuz closure disrupting Gulf installations, while perovskite-silicon tandem cells remain a 2028+ commercialisation target.

themes.theme: US Power Demand Revision Lifts Solar Stakes

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie revised US power demand growth forecasts upward from 2.9% to 3.2% per year over the next decade in its H1 2026 North America Power Markets Strategic Planning Outlook.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: Repeated upward revisions to demand growth compound the capacity shortfall, directly expanding the total addressable market for solar and storage developers.
insights.quote: US power demand growth forecasts have been revised upward again in Wood Mackenzie's latest North America Power Markets Strategic Planning Outlook for H1 2026 - from 2.9% to 3.2% over the next decade.
insights.locator: Solar and the demand surge section

insights.claim: Data centres are now driving two-thirds of US load growth, creating sustained and intensifying pressure on generation capacity additions.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: Data centre dominance of load growth concentrates large-scale power procurement decisions among a small set of hyperscaler buyers, giving those buyers outsized influence over project economics.
insights.quote: Data centres are now driving two-thirds of that load growth, creating sustained and intensifying pressure on generation capacity additions.
insights.locator: Solar and the demand surge section

insights.claim: Solar is expected to add 465 TWh of additional generation capacity in the US between 2026 and 2035, maintaining its position as the second-largest source of new generation behind gas.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: Despite gas retaining the top slot, solar's 465 TWh pipeline is a multi-hundred-billion-dollar investment opportunity over the next decade.
insights.quote: Solar continues to hold its position as the second largest source of new generation behind gas, with 465 TWh of additional capacity expected between 2026 and 2035.
insights.locator: Solar and the demand surge section

insights.claim: Solar's cost competitiveness and shorter deployment timelines give it a structural advantage in a US market that urgently needs fast capacity additions.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: Speed-to-capacity is a differentiating factor when grid tightness is acute — investors in solar developers benefit from this structural tailwind vs. longer-lead alternatives.
insights.quote: Its cost competitiveness and shorter deployment timelines give it a structural advantage in a market that needs capacity fast.
insights.locator: Solar and the demand surge section

themes.theme: Solar Technology Transition and the Silver Crisis

insights.claim: TOPCon is today's mainstream solar cell technology and is expected to remain dominant through at least 2028, but N-type cells are approaching their theoretical efficiency limits with only around 1.5% of headroom remaining.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: TOPCon dominance through 2028 sets the commercial window during which silver-intensity economics will weigh on module cost structures.
insights.quote: TOPCon is today's mainstream technology and is expected to remain dominant through at least 2028. But N-type cells are approaching their theoretical efficiency limits, with only around 1.5% of headroom remaining.
insights.locator: Solar technology at a crossroads section

insights.claim: Perovskite-silicon tandem cells have achieved lab-scale efficiencies exceeding 34%, but commercialisation at scale is not expected before 2028, with heat and moisture stability remaining critical constraints.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: The 2028+ commercialisation timeline means investors cannot count on next-generation efficiency gains to offset near-term silver cost pressure; incumbent TOPCon manufacturers retain pricing power for now.
insights.quote: lab-scale efficiencies have already exceeded 34%, but commercialisation at scale is not expected before 2028, with heat and moisture stability still a critical constraint.
insights.locator: Solar technology at a crossroads section

insights.claim: Silver now accounts for 25% of TOPCon cell costs after silver prices more than doubled, pushing total cell costs up more than 30% in the past year.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: A 30%+ cell cost increase driven by a single commodity is a significant margin risk for module manufacturers and a procurement risk for project developers locked into fixed-price EPC contracts.
insights.quote: Silver now accounts for 25% of TOPCon cell costs after prices more than doubled, pushing total cell costs up more than 30% in the past year.
insights.locator: Solar technology at a crossroads section

insights.claim: Reducing silver consumption in solar cells has shifted from a cost optimisation goal to a strategic necessity, accelerating innovation in busbar design and silver-copper substitution.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: Companies with proprietary low-silver or copper-substituted cell processes are gaining a durable cost advantage that could reorder competitive rankings among module manufacturers.
insights.quote: Reducing silver consumption has shifted from a cost optimisation goal to a strategic necessity, accelerating innovation in busbar design and silver-copper substitution.
insights.locator: Solar technology at a crossroads section

themes.theme: Geopolitical Disruption and Regional Outlook Risks

insights.claim: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has driven a sharp reduction in near-term solar installation forecasts in the Middle East, with multiple gigawatts of projects delayed or cancelled.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: Middle East solar project delays remove GW-scale near-term demand for panels and EPC services — a direct revenue risk for module suppliers and developers exposed to Gulf markets.
insights.quote: the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has driven a sharp reduction in near-term installation forecasts, with multiple gigawatts of projects delayed or cancelled
insights.locator: Also in 'Global solar: key themes through H1 2026' section

insights.claim: More than half of all US states are now actively considering enabling legislation for balcony solar, but organised labour opposition is proving a more significant obstacle than anticipated in some markets.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: Labour opposition introduces a non-regulatory political risk that could slow residential distributed solar adoption even in politically supportive states.
insights.quote: More than half of all US states are now actively considering enabling legislation, but organised labour opposition is proving a more significant obstacle than anticipated in some markets.
insights.locator: Also in 'Global solar: key themes through H1 2026' section

insights.claim: The first half of 2026 has been marked by upward demand revisions, more urgent technology roadmaps, and geopolitical shocks that tested solar supply chains in ways few anticipated at the start of the year.
insights.speaker: Michelle Davis
insights.so_what: The combination of higher demand and supply-chain fragility is a classic setup for margin volatility — investors should monitor module spot pricing and developer backlog quality closely.
insights.quote: Demand forecasts have moved higher, technology roadmaps have grown more urgent, and geopolitical shocks have tested supply chains in ways that few anticipated at the start of the year.
